resin_codes.name: PET
resin_codes.why: PET is the most commonly recycled plastic globally. When recycled, it reduces petroleum extraction and can be turned into polyester fiber for clothing and carpets.
resin_codes.action: Rinse thoroughly and place in the blue dry waste bin. Crush the bottle to save space.
resin_codes.contamination: Food or oil residue contaminates the recycling batch. Always rinse before disposal.

resin_codes.name: HDPE
resin_codes.why: HDPE is durable and resistant to chemicals. Recycling HDPE saves significant energy compared to producing new plastic.
resin_codes.action: Rinse and place in blue bin. Remove caps if they are a different resin code.
resin_codes.contamination: Leftover product can degrade the recycled material quality.

resin_codes.name: PVC
resin_codes.why: PVC contains chlorine and toxic additives. It releases dioxins when burned and cannot be safely recycled in standard facilities.
resin_codes.action: Do NOT place in standard bins. Take to specialized e-waste or hazardous waste collection centers.
resin_codes.contamination: Never burn PVC. It releases toxic hydrochloric acid fumes.

resin_codes.name: LDPE
resin_codes.why: LDPE is lightweight and flexible. It can be recycled into trash can liners, lumber, and floor tiles.
resin_codes.action: Clean and place in blue bin. Bundle plastic bags together to prevent them from clogging machinery.
resin_codes.contamination: Food-soiled bags cannot be recycled. Rinse or discard if heavily soiled.

resin_codes.name: PP
resin_codes.why: PP has a high melting point and is used for hot-fill products. It is increasingly accepted in recycling programs.
resin_codes.action: Rinse and place in blue bin. Check for recycling symbol #5 on the bottom.
resin_codes.contamination: Food residue, especially dairy, can cause odors and attract pests. Rinse well.

resin_codes.name: PS
resin_codes.why: Polystyrene is brittle, lightweight, and breaks into microplastics easily. It is rarely recycled due to economic and technical challenges.
resin_codes.action: Place in red bin for landfill. Avoid using disposable thermocol whenever possible.
resin_codes.contamination: Food-soiled thermocol cannot be recycled and should go to landfill.

resin_codes.name: Other
resin_codes.why: Category 7 is a catch-all for plastics that do not fit categories 1-6. Most are not recyclable through standard municipal programs.
resin_codes.action: Check local guidelines. Most go to red bin. Some bioplastics require industrial composting.
resin_codes.contamination: Mixed plastics are nearly impossible to separate and recycle.

quiz.question: Which bin does a used Bisleri bottle go in?
quiz.explanation: PET bottles are recyclable dry waste. Rinse and place in the blue bin.

quiz.question: A dahi container with residue goes where?
quiz.explanation: PP containers are recyclable, but dairy residue contaminates batches. Rinse thoroughly before blue bin.

quiz.question: What is the resin code for a plastic carry bag?
quiz.explanation: Most plastic carry bags are LDPE (code #4). Bundle them before placing in the blue bin.

quiz.question: A thermocol plate from a street vendor goes in?
quiz.explanation: Polystyrene (PS, code #6) is non-recyclable in most Indian cities. It goes to the red bin for landfill.

quiz.question: A PVC pipe fragment should be disposed of?
quiz.explanation: PVC requires specialized handling. Never burn it (releases toxic fumes). Use designated e-waste or hazardous centers.
